sentences.text: You're wrong.
Edna, you said, Edna.
Enid.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We went back to the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH].
Where we-- where we worked.
No, in the farm.
I worked in there as a house matron, you know, as a maid.
And other people worked on the farm.
And then we moved from that farm to a smaller farm.
But it was all freedom, no more German.
I didn't work.
At the farm?
No, we-- nobody worked.
You were on-- on your own, after the war.
Yeah.
You were really on your own.
Polish family, yeah.
Of course, so I was with them.
Where they went, we went.
And then I decided, after a while being with them, and I had got a little older.
I got to understand, you know, you got a [? boyfriend ?] .
You know, I said to my Polish lady, I would like to go and look and see if I can find Jewish people.
Because I think I'm not the only one in Europe.
So, she says, so where do you want to go?
I said, I think the biggest-- to a big city, like Lodz.
I don't know how come I didn't say Warsaw.
I said Lodz.
I didn't know what-- so she said, but you can't go alone.
Because this is dangerous for you, you know, there were the-- the Russian army does-- can do to you that you're alone.
They were going after girls.
I said, I know.
But I have to do that.
She said, if you want to do it, I'll go with you.
So, I said, OK, if you want to do that.
So we both dressed up.
So she left her family to go with you?
She left her husband and the children, yeah.
And she went with me.
And we went to Lodz by train.
And when we arrived to Lodz, it was a rainy day.
We didn't know where we're going.
We walked down the street.
We didn't know Lodz.
She didn't know Lodz.
I didn't know Lodz.
So we figured-- we figured we'll go to the Jewish office.
There must be a Jewish office.
Don't ask me.
I don't know.
I think she paid.
Or maybe we traveled for free.
I don't know.
I had no money.
The clothes what I had, from after the-- the war ended, we took some clothes from people that left the homes.
Yeah.
The-- the Russian soldier said, do whatever you want for three days.
You can take anything out, whatever is in the house left, whatever it's good for you, you want it, take it.
All the Germans, yeah.
The Germans did not come back.
I don't know where they went.
They didn't come back.
Maybe they come back-- came back later.
But the first three days, no.
It's a small town, just small--
Yeah, like-- sort of like-- yeah, where I was liberated.
So I picked-- you know, we went over to the houses, and whatever, shoes, this.
No, no.
This we didn't need.
What we needed is just food and clothes.
[LAUGHS]
No, not to get rich on somebody, no.
No, I mean what are we going to-- we had no place to be anyway.
We had to go back and look for a place.
So, no, no, just what we needed.
And not much, a dress or so, a pair of shoes, if they fitted me, you know.
And something when we left, when we left whatever I had in Germany, you know?
What the German lady used to give me for Christmas gifts and this.
I used to have a dress, she used to give me some shoes from her.
I had a little bundle of my own.
No.
Never saw her again, no.
The Jewish Office.
But-- but-- so we went, we walk in the street.
And it was raining, drizzling sort of like.
And there we see three men walking towards us.
And they stopped us.
And they were holding umbrellas.
And they stopped us, and they said to us, they saw we two-- a mother and the daughter probably, because walking with the-- babushkas, you know.
So they stopped, and they asked us if we would like to buy an umbrella.
And I said, maybe.
But before we decide to buy an umbrella, I want to ask you a question.
They look to me Jewish.
So I said to them, are you Jewish?
They were ready to kill me.
They thought I'm Polish.
What am I, an anti-Semite?
They said, why are you asking that question?
I said, because I'm Jewish.
They says, what?
And what about this lady?
I said, that lady's not Jewish.
But thanks to this lovely lady and her family, I'm-- I survived as a Polish.
She-- they couldn't believe me.
So, OK.
How can I believe you that you are not-- that you're Jewish?
You speak Jewish?
I said, yes.
So I started to speak Jewish, it came out German, because I spent most of time in Germany.
So they said, this is not Jewish.
This is German.
What else do you speak?
Do you know the Jewish holidays?
I said, yeah.
Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, and things like that.
All right.
Even Polacks know, Polish people know that, Ukrainian.
Do you know how to write in Jewish?
I said, yes.
I think I still remember.
Because I had a tutor at home, and we were-- we were very Jewish.
So they gave me a pencil.
And there was a window sill, with no window.
And they told me what to write.
So I did.
And that what's made them-- Believe.
--believe that I'm Jewish.
Because with no other people-- Polish, Ukrainian-- knew how to write Yiddish, Jewish.
So those three men at that time also returned from the war as survivors.
As a matter of fact, that's my husband-- one of them, my husband knew him very well.
One name was [NON-ENGLISH], like I don't remember the first name.
The other one was [NON-ENGLISH].

That's why I said, the Polish name, I forgot-- the German guy, you asked me the German's second name, and I don't remember if it was-- it was [NON-ENGLISH] or was-- anyway, that name from the Jewish guy was [NON-ENGLISH].
And the third one, I really don't remember.
He was so-- so-- so, they took us into their place, they had a little tiny room.
No, we never get to the office.
Because they were so excited.
And they--
Yeah, they had-- yeah.
And-- and they said, so what do you want to do?
And I said, I would like to be with Jewish people.
So he said, you know what?
They said to me, we are getting ready to go further-- to Germany.
We don't want to stay here in Poland, because they don't like us here anymore.
And we'll go from Germany, and maybe from Germany we'll go to United States, or maybe to Israel, wherever.
Israel wasn't Israel yet then.
Palestine, yeah.
So that's what we planning to do.
So, yes.
So wait.
So I said, so, if you want to go, when are you going?
A day-- in a day or two.
But, he said, you're lucky.
There was one woman who was supposed to come with us.
We made out papers for her and for us, decided she's not going.
So if you want to come with us, you'll be-- you'll be-- you'll take over the papers, her papers.
I had no papers.
See, the German people had to make papers out.
But they didn't give it to us.
All the papers they made, they kept in that house where I worked in Germany.
Everything was in their office.
We didn't-- who would look for paper?
So anyway, I said to them, you know, I have to go back with the Polish lady.
I have some belongings to take home-- to take with me.
Otherwise, I only have what-- what I wear.
To her-- not-- just her family, yeah.
That means-- I say, Polish lady, is the family included.
And I took my bundle, and we went back to Lodz.
And from Lodz, we were going further to-- I was going with them to Munich--
Yeah.
Yeah, not only they-- most of us.
I guess after the war, when the was over, the Polacks didn't want Jewish people in the-- in their country anymore.
Well, we figured that-- I don't know what made us really to think.
But that's where we went.
And from there, we had hopes to go to United States.
This was just like a stop-over.
And this is the way it was.
Don't ask me why.
So-- so we went back.
And we went, and we took the train.
And I was crying like a baby for my family that I left behind, the Polish family.
Yeah, I went.
I didn't-- they didn't give it to me.
But when we stopped at the border, you know, we had to show papers.
So they showed the papers with the man.
I had no papers on me.
I was not-- I don't know.
No.
I didn't pay attention to this.
Who cared?
There was no such-- it wasn't normal.
And it had nothing-- even after the war was not a normal life, until it stabilized itself.
So-- so what?
So I-- yes.
So we sat down on the train.
And I sat, but then the train came-- came in.
So I was sitting in the-- [NON-ENGLISH] they called it.
And I cried.
Station.
And I was writing letters.
And I didn't know what I was writing.
And I was sending letters.
And I was, like, why did I do that?
I wanted to be with you all the time.
You were so good to me.
And I don't think she ever received the letter.
Because later on, when I wrote-- I don't know how I find her later.
I didn't really have-- I don't think I-- maybe I had her address, I must have.
So anyway, and we were going into Germany.
So we-- the first stop was Berlin.
We came into Berlin.
Everybody knew Berlin, there was some Jewish people left.
And they had a-- a [NON-ENGLISH], so this is an office.
A committee?
A committee, office place.
There was one building that everybody who came in from the survivors to Berlin, stopped off in that office.
First of all, we needed cards to go out to eat, to have-- to eat, because we really didn't have so much money.
I didn't.
And-- and from there, we were going on.
So we stopped in this Jewish committee.
I really don't know.
Maybe my husband would know better.
That's where I met my husband.
So when we came-- go, when we came to that-- to that street and to that building, my husband was outside.
He came like a day before with his sister and brother-in-law from Lodz.
So-- and he recognized the three men that I came with.
So he started to talk to them.
And they-- then he said, then he asked them, who-- who was she?
Yeah.
No, I wasn't so blonde.
[LAUGHTER] I was a little darker than blonde.
[LAUGHS] So she said-- they said, the story.
They tell him the story.
Then we went upstairs.
I met his sister and his brother-in-law.
And this is where we stayed like a few days.
And my husband started to-- for the night it was around evening already, that if I would like to go to the movie with him in Berlin.
So I said, OK.
What do I care?
I go.
[LAUGHTER] So I went with them to a movie.
Then we came from the movie.
Tell us his name.
Whose?
My date?
Lazer-- Lazer-- Leon.
Leon, we call him Leon.
Polish is Leon.
And that's what they called him.
But his-- his Jewish name is [NON-ENGLISH].
Chencinski.
[LAUGHS] So anyway, and then, little by little, he sort of liked me.
I don't know.
And so the next day again, we went out together.
His sister wasn't even so happy because she wanted [LAUGHTER] him to go with her and her brother-in-law.
So like I say, [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
So anyway-- Yeah.
She wants to drag along a little caboose.
But see, and then after two, three days they send us to a-- to a camp, which was a little further, a little-- it was on, I think, the Russian side.
Because Berlin was divided on three or four sides, it was the Russian-- the-- we were I think on the-- on the American zone or the British zone.
Then comes the French zone.
There was the British zone, then was the Russian zone.
So it was, I think-- now I'm not sure, if-- if it was the British or the Russian or something.
The British must be.
Anyway, we stayed.
It's like not really a DP camp, but it was like just a temporary-- a [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] camp is?
Yeah.
But we stayed there.
They had-- they had home buildings there.
I think it was from the-- that soldiers used to stay there in the--
Barracks, yeah.
Yeah-- they give us--
I don't-- maybe my husband will know.
I don't know.
I know we-- we were-- we stayed.
We had a room.
They gave us one room.
The three men and me.
And then-- then a day later, my husband was there too, came with his sister and his brother-in-law.
So we met again.
And then-- then we-- every morning we got up, we went to breakfast.
And every morning it was-- it was-- they put out a sign who's going today, leaving further, and going back to-- further on to Germany, into Germany, and who's not.
So one morning, we went in.
We went for breakfast.
And we saw the three-- the four of us name on the list, not my husband.
And the night before was like a good-bye party and so.
So my husband find some-- a woman, a girl, a young girl who know how to dance.
So he got involved with her, and we went home.
So-- Just let me ask you this.
When you were there and you saw a list of names, did you ever make an attempt to find out about your own real family?
No.
Not then.
Not then.
I could not.
I saw my family in Stepan.
I know they went to the-- and Stepan was far away from Berlin, far away.
I really, the honest truth, we talked about the family.
But the question was--
I didn't even try.
I don't think so.
Because actually, maybe now of course, of course now-- if-- it wasn't really normal or anything.
You know, you were here today, tomorrow in another place.
And then all of a sudden, you stopped.
So what we-- when we went-- my husband was-- he was still-- left-- he was left behind.
But with the three men and I, we went on.
And we stopped.
We supposedly were-- I told him that we're planning to stop in Munich.
But meanwhile we stopped in the English zone in Hanover.
And then we went also to the Jewish committee, to-- to sign in, and then they gave us-- they gave us stamps, sort of like to go and to eat, to get-- to eat, to get food.
And they gave us--
In a restaurant.
Yeah.
It was everything-- Germany was already, you could go in and buy.
There was restaurants.
Yeah.
It was-- it was a little-- it was not so-- that was not really like after the war.
It took a few months after that when we got there.
So-- so-- and every-- so yeah, and they gave us room where to stay.
I think it was summertime already.
It was not hot.
But it was a real-- maybe summertime or so.
And then my husband came the next day.
And I was inside, in the office to give-- to tell my story like I'm telling you now.
Because we had to register.
And they had to know exactly where I was-- how I survived, where I survived, and what survived.
Maybe in that time, not with all the details.
And I explained how I survived in Germany as a Polish girl, and this and that, everything.
And they gave me a card.
And I registered.
And that's it.
And when I walked out from the office--
I registered Henya.
Yeah, my real name.
Henya Jukin, my real name, no more--
Fake, everything was real.
So, so my-- so my brother-in-law now, he saw me coming out.
So he ran, and he embraced me.
He said-- they called me Genya.
Because in Polish, it's Henya, Genya, the Henya-- Henya, Genya, Henya!
Everything like this.
Guess what?
We all here.
Leon is here.
And my-- and-- I mean, my wife is here.
So we met again.
Because, I said--
He said to me, you were supposed to stop in-- in Munich, and he says, now you're here?
I said, well, didn't you talk about Munich?
Everybody talked about Munich.
And this was the stop.
And that's where we-- and here we are.
And we-- we remained in Germany till-- until in Hanover.
We never went back to Munich-- I was in Munich.
But not-- and then we-- I started-- we started to date each other for real.
Do you remember what year that was?
That was '45 for real, and we were married in '46.
In Germany-- still in Germany.
Yeah, in Hanover.
First off, we had everything-- the UNRRA gave us-- supported us.
We got support from the UNRRA.
So we really didn't have anything to do.
Oh, we had a good time.
We went to movies.
And we went to-- and then I get-- got pregnant.
And I have my son.
So I was busy with my son.
And it was like normal life.
And we kept on talking from all those years, what we went through for the-- you know, we left the family, and we lost everybody.
And I'm alone.
And not too many people are left-- were left all alone.
There are families that had-- like my husband, for instance, he survived.
His-- his three sisters survived.
He only lost his father from starvation in the ghetto.
And his mother went in the oven with his niece, little niece, and his brother was also-- his younger brother didn't survive, which he'll show you.
But then more and more people survived, find family, a sister, a brother, and some nobody.
And I was no.
I survived with nobody.
Yeah, there was a committee-- a committee, Jewish committee.
And we registered before going.
Then came-- now then it came-- the soldiers came in to see-- Jewish soldiers, British.
They sort of come into our place, and they would-- wanted to know how we survived and what.
And they asked us--
No.
And we asked him-- well, there was-- maybe was one guy special that he was questioning a lot.
Because he was Jewish himself.
And he wanted to help us.
So he said to us, do you have any relatives in United States or in Britain, or so?
Or in Israel?
So I said, yeah.
I have relatives in the United States.
And that's where I would like to go.
So he said, do you know their names?
I said, yes.
I have an uncle by the name Sol Wetizman.
That's my mother's brother.
And my mother's sister is Kaila Geller.
Geller, yeah.
Oh, they came before, after the World War I.
They went to America.
My uncle was 14 years old, and his sister.
My mother was supposed to go, but she had some of the [NON-ENGLISH] kids, that they're, you know, they say, where we are we going to go?
And the kid stayed where the mother stayed.
She had really papers and the war broke out.
Somewhere it was World War II, or I, or II; I, I think.
I don't know.
I'm mixed up.
Anyway, so this is-- the-- so he gave-- he took the names, and he gave these-- the names into the the Jewish And it went to United States.
And my uncle told us-- just recently he told me that day, the phone did not stop ringing.
Everybody-- my uncle-- my uncle was known in Brooklyn.
Everybody-- he was the president of the society.
Stepan society.
Everybody called, if he knows if he saw on the paper that there's a survivor from his family that has survived.
So-- it was-- it was something.
[CRYING] Sorry.
I [CRYING] didn't think I'll do that.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Anyway, [INAUDIBLE] he started to write to me letters.
I give him the address that this is where-- you know, they had [NON-ENGLISH].
Excuse me.
And-- and he kept on writing, and I kept writing back.
And then he made papers for us to come.
At that time, I told him I have a boyfriend-- I have a boyfriend, and then he became a husband.
And then I-- by the time the papers came, that I had everything there.
And then I had a baby.
And I said, first of all to him, I can't come with-- by myself.
Because I have a boyfriend.
And then I write him, that I got married.
It took so long.
It was like a couple years.
We were married in-- in-- '46.
We were-- we came to the United States, it was '49, a few years.
So-- and that was-- that's the way it happened.
No.
No.
And don't ask me why.
Because I really don't-- cannot explain it to you.
Yeah, survivors.
Some lived-- we stayed in the community.
And other people lived outside the community, like in private homes, private apartments, you know.
And all this the UNRRA took care.
And they gives us some money sometimes and this.
A little, here and there.
Yeah, my husband was a policeman.
For the community, for this.
No, just for that little house, it was one house or so.
I-- I really-- I just survived.
Just it was I was one of the lucky ones.
Yeah, yeah.
And my-- my sister and brothers were there, and they didn't do that.
Or maybe they did and they caught them.
It's something I never know.

Then I met people like after-- years later, we met people from Israel, when I went in '63, and they told me that they find out that this one, my brother they caught-- my brother going on the way-- going to Gestapo.
And they killed him.
And my mother was killed someplace else.
But this is all talk.
And nobody saw it.
Just because from mouth to mouth, you know.
So-- and-- that's what I can tell you.
No.
They were so nice though, like different people, not like the people from-- during the war, different people.
Yeah, yeah.
We were again-- before I met my husband-- before my husband, before we really got married, I stayed privately in a room.
With a German in a German's house.
Nightmares all the time.
But in all the time when we got together, we talked about the-- everybody talked about their life, how they survived.
And the only thing which kept us-- to be happy is that we saw the end of Hitler, that he-- we survived.
He was-- he was going to kill all of us.
With Hitler, there will be no survivors.
But somehow, thanks to God, there were survivors.
And he-- he didn't survive.
So we saw, like we say in Jewish, the [NON-ENGLISH].
From--
The downfall from Hitler and Germany.
